name: org.machanism.machai:genai-client
description: GenAI Client is a Java library in the Machai ecosystem that provides a provider-agnostic integration layer for generative AI and embedding services. It helps applications interact with multiple AI backends through one consistent API for prompt execution, system instructions, file and URL context, host-side function tools, provider resolution, and token-usage reporting. The library centers on the org.machanism.machai.ai root API, the org.machanism.machai.ai.manager package for provider lookup and usage aggregation, the org.machanism.machai.ai.provider package for common contracts and shared abstractions, concrete provider implementations for OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, and EPAM CodeMie, and the org.machanism.machai.ai.tools package for discovering and registering Java methods as AI-callable tools and prompts. Key capabilities include resolving providers from identifiers like OpenAI:gpt-4o-mini or Claude:claude-3-5-sonnet, initializing them from runtime configuration, attaching instructions and prompts, registering local tools through FunctionToolsLoader and ToolFunction callbacks, executing iterative tool-call loops, enabling optional built-in web search and MCP server integrations, generating embeddings for semantic search and similarity workflows, and aggregating immutable token-usage metrics with Usage and UsageStatistics. OpenAIProvider supports OpenAI-compatible Responses API request construction, function-tool calling, file inputs, embedding generation, usage capture, web search, and MCP tools. AnthropicProvider supports Anthropic Beta Messages API execution, custom function tools, web search, MCP server forwarding, and usage tracking. CodeMieProvider authenticates against an OAuth 2.0 token endpoint and delegates supported model families to OpenAI-compatible or Anthropic-compatible providers. ToolsProvider supplies a structured YAML-based tool execution mode for host-defined tools. The library is designed for request-scoped workflows in Java applications that need semantic search, intelligent project assembly, automated content generation, prompt orchestration, and controlled tool invocation without binding application code directly to a single vendor SDK.

usageContext: Java applications that need a unified client for multiple GenAI providers
Machai modules that perform prompt execution, embedding generation, or intelligent project assembly
Backend services that need request-scoped AI provider instances with runtime configuration
Applications that expose host-side Java tools to AI models through structured tool calling
Systems that aggregate token usage for monitoring and reporting
Integrations that require OpenAI-compatible, Anthropic Claude, or EPAM CodeMie access through a common API

constructors.signature: public static org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai getProvider(java.lang.String chatModel, org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf)
constructors.description: Primary factory-style entry point for creating and initializing a concrete generative AI provider from a provider/model identifier. The method resolves simple names using the Machai naming convention or accepts a fully qualified provider class name, applies the selected model to runtime configuration, instantiates the provider, and returns a ready-to-use Genai implementation.

examples.description: Resolves an OpenAI-backed provider from a Provider:Model identifier, adds instructions and a user prompt, and performs a text-generation request using the common provider API.

constructors.description: Factory-style entry point for creating a provider that supports embedding generation. It resolves the provider class from the given identifier, initializes it with runtime configuration, and returns an EmbeddingProvider for semantic-search and similarity workflows.

examples.description: Creates an embedding-capable provider from a provider/model identifier and generates a vector for downstream semantic search or similarity ranking.

constructors.description: Direct constructor for the OpenAI-backed provider implementation. Use it when application code wants explicit control over the concrete provider type before calling init, then add prompts, tools, web-search support, MCP servers, or embedding requests.

examples.description: Instantiates the OpenAI provider directly, initializes it from configuration, and executes a prompt using the OpenAI-compatible Responses API adapter.

constructors.description: Direct constructor for the Anthropic Claude provider implementation. This is appropriate when the application explicitly targets Claude models and wants to configure tool calling, web search, MCP servers, and usage capture through the Anthropic-backed implementation.

examples.description: Creates a Claude-backed provider explicitly and sends a prompt through the common Genai lifecycle using Anthropic configuration.

constructors.description: Direct constructor for the EPAM CodeMie integration. The provider authenticates with a CodeMie OpenID Connect token endpoint, retrieves an OAuth 2.0 bearer token, and delegates requests to an OpenAI-compatible or Anthropic-compatible downstream provider depending on the configured model family.

examples.description: Builds a CodeMie-backed provider, authenticates through the configured token endpoint during initialization, and delegates the prompt to a compatible downstream provider.

constructors.description: Direct constructor for the tools-oriented provider implementation. This provider exposes registered Java tool functions and can interpret the last prompt as a YAML-based tool invocation when configured for YAML execution mode.

examples.description: Creates the internal tools provider and submits a YAML tool-call description so the registered host-side tool can be invoked through the same provider lifecycle.

constructors.description: Creates a ServiceLoader-based tool installer that discovers FunctionTools implementations from the classpath and applies compatible tool and prompt definitions to a Genai provider. This is the main object-construction step for enabling host-side tools dynamically.

examples.description: Creates the loader responsible for discovering host-side FunctionTools implementations and registering their tools and prompts with a provider for a specific application class.

features.name: getProvider
features.signature: public static org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai getProvider(java.lang.String chatModel, org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf)
features.description: Resolves and initializes a concrete Genai implementation from a Provider:Model identifier. This is the primary feature for provider-agnostic AI request execution because it hides provider class selection, naming conventions, and initialization details from application code.

examples.description: Demonstrates how an application can switch provider backends by changing only the provider/model identifier while keeping the same Genai usage pattern.

features.name: getEmbeddingProvider
features.description: Creates an embedding-capable provider for semantic-search, similarity, and natural-language-processing workflows. It abstracts provider-specific embedding setup behind one common API.

examples.description: Shows a practical semantic-search setup where the application resolves an embedding provider from configuration and requests a numeric vector for indexing.

features.name: perform
features.description: Core execution feature of the Genai contract. Implementations send the accumulated prompts, instructions, files, and registered tools to the selected provider and return the generated text response. Concrete providers may internally handle multi-step tool-call loops before returning the final text.

examples.description: Basic request-scoped usage of the provider lifecycle: configure instructions and prompt, execute the provider, consume the response, then clear state before reuse.

features.name: embedding
features.signature: public abstract java.util.List<java.lang.Double> embedding(java.lang.String text, long dimensions)
features.description: Primary embedding-generation feature exposed by EmbeddingProvider implementations such as OpenAIProvider and CodeMieProvider. It computes a numeric vector for the given input text and requested dimensionality, enabling semantic search, clustering, and similarity scoring.

examples.snippet:
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider provider = null;
java.util.List<java.lang.Double> vector = provider.embedding("customer support article", 384L);
examples.description: A practical how-to-use-it scenario for building semantic-search indexes: send each document or snippet through the embedding method and store the resulting vector in the search index.

features.name: addTools
features.description: Registers annotated Java tool methods with a provider so the model can invoke controlled host-side capabilities during response generation. This is a key feature for retrieval, automation, and domain-specific task execution.

examples.description: Illustrates how application-defined FunctionTools can be attached to a provider before execution so the model can trigger host-side logic during the request.

features.name: addPrompts
features.description: Discovers methods annotated with Prompt in a FunctionTools implementation and registers them for role-aware prompt injection. This enables reusable prompt catalogs and application-specific assistant or user message generation.

examples.description: Shows how reusable prompt definitions from a FunctionTools implementation can be made available to a provider alongside tool functions.

features.name: applyTools
features.description: ServiceLoader-based bulk registration feature that discovers all FunctionTools implementations on the classpath, filters them by SupportedFor compatibility, and applies both tool functions and prompts to the target provider. This is the standard runtime bootstrap for host-side tool ecosystems.

examples.description: Practical setup step for applications that want automatic discovery and registration of all compatible tool packs instead of manually wiring each one.

features.name: perform
features.description: Executes an OpenAI-compatible Responses API request using the configured model, accumulated inputs, and registered tools. If the response contains tool calls, the provider invokes the matching local tools and continues the request cycle until final text is produced.

examples.description: How to use the OpenAI implementation directly for provider-specific orchestration while still relying on the shared Machai provider abstraction.

features.name: perform
features.description: Executes an Anthropic Beta Messages API request with the accumulated conversation state, custom tools, optional web search, and optional MCP servers. If Anthropic returns tool-use blocks, the provider resolves the tool loop before returning the final plain-text response.

examples.description: How to execute a Claude-backed request directly when the application explicitly targets Anthropic models and needs tool-use loop support.

features.name: embedding
features.signature: public java.util.List<java.lang.Double> embedding(java.lang.String text, long dimensions)
features.description: OpenAI-backed embedding request feature. It turns text into a vector using the configured embedding model and returns the values as a Java list of doubles for downstream indexing or similarity computation.

examples.description: Direct embedding usage against an OpenAI-compatible backend for a semantic-search indexing pipeline.

features.name: getToken
features.signature: public static java.lang.String getToken(java.lang.String url, java.lang.String username, java.lang.String password) throws java.io.IOException
features.description: CodeMie authentication feature that obtains an OAuth 2.0 access token from a token endpoint using either password grant or client-credentials grant, depending on the username format. This enables secure delegation to hosted downstream model providers.

examples.snippet: java.lang.String token = org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.codemie.CodeMieProvider.getToken("https://auth.example/token", "user@example.com", "secret");
examples.description: Shows the authentication step used before CodeMie-backed requests are delegated to an OpenAI-compatible or Anthropic-compatible provider.

features.name: perform
features.description: Structured host-tool execution feature provided by ToolsProvider. When the chat model is configured for YAML mode, the last prompt is interpreted as a YAML tool-call description that names a tool and passes parameters, allowing deterministic host-side execution without an external AI backend.

examples.description: A practical how-to-use-it scenario for invoking a registered host tool through YAML-structured input, useful for deterministic orchestration or testing.

features.name: addUsage
features.signature: public static void addUsage(java.lang.String modelId, org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.Usage usage)
features.description: Records immutable usage metrics for a specific model identifier. This supports centralized monitoring of token consumption across many provider calls in a larger workflow.

examples.description: Demonstrates how to store and later report token-consumption metrics for operational monitoring and cost visibility.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools
customizations.description: Primary extension point for defining host-side tool collections. Implementations expose local capabilities such as file access, command execution, HTTP retrieval, or domain-specific operations that can later be discovered and registered with AI providers.

examples.description: How to create a custom FunctionTools implementation that exposes deterministic host-side capabilities to AI providers using the annotation model defined by the library.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.Tool
customizations.description: Annotation-based customization point for exposing a Java method as an AI-callable tool. It lets developers define the tool name and human-readable description used by the provider during tool registration and model reasoning.

examples.description: Shows how developers customize the tool catalog by annotating a plain Java method so it becomes available for model-driven invocation.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.Prompt
customizations.description: Annotation-based extension point for reusable prompt definitions. Developers can mark methods as prompt providers, describe their purpose, and associate them with assistant or user roles for runtime registration.

examples.description: Demonstrates how prompt definitions can be externalized into reusable annotated methods and later discovered by the provider runtime.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.SupportedFor
customizations.description: Compatibility filter for FunctionTools implementations. Developers annotate a tool collection with SupportedFor to restrict automatic registration to specific application classes or processors when multiple applications share the same runtime.

examples.description: Illustrates how tool packs can be scoped to selected application classes so only compatible providers or processors receive them during auto-registration.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.GenaiAdapter
customizations.description: Decorator and wrapper customization point for Genai implementations. Applications can subclass or use the adapter pattern to add logging, retries, metrics, request shaping, or other cross-cutting concerns while preserving the common Genai contract.

examples.description: How to wrap an existing provider with a delegating adapter so additional behavior can be added without changing caller code that depends on the Genai interface.

customizations.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.AbstractAIProvider
customizations.description: Base-class extension point for implementing new provider backends. Subclasses inherit common configuration handling, request logging, tool invocation safety, MCP and web-search bootstrap support, timeout handling, and usage-accounting state.

examples.description: Demonstrates the shape of a custom provider integration that reuses the shared Machai provider infrastructure while implementing backend-specific tool registration and execution.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai
studs.description: Primary provider contract for generative-AI integrations. It defines the common lifecycle for initialization, prompt and instruction collection, host-tool registration, execution, state clearing, input logging, and project-directory propagation.

examples.description: Shows the central application-facing contract in use: resolve a provider, add request context, execute, and consume the answer through the stable Genai API.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider
studs.description: Contract for providers that can generate embedding vectors. It standardizes initialization and vector generation for semantic-search and similarity-based workflows.

examples.snippet:
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.EmbeddingProvider provider = null;
java.util.List<java.lang.Double> vector = provider.embedding("example text", 384L);
examples.description: Illustrates the minimal contract needed to transform text into an embedding vector for indexing or similarity scoring.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools
studs.description: Marker SPI for tool-installer classes that define local capabilities such as file access, command execution, HTTP retrieval, or domain-specific operations to be registered with a Genai provider.

examples.snippet:
public final class ProjectTools implements org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools {
}
examples.description: Shows the starting point for implementing a tool pack that can later be discovered by FunctionToolsLoader and attached to a provider.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.ToolFunction
studs.description: Functional interface representing executable tool logic. Implementations accept structured JSON parameters, the provider working directory, and runtime configuration, then return a result object suitable for serialization back into the model conversation.
methods: java.lang.Object apply(com.fasterxml.jackson.databind.JsonNode params, java.io.File projectDir, org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator config) throws java.io.IOException

examples.snippet: org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.ToolFunction tool = (params, projectDir, config) -> "ok";
examples.description: Demonstrates the lambda-friendly execution contract used by provider integrations when a model requests a local host-side tool invocation.

examples.title: Use a provider-agnostic GenAI client for prompt execution
examples.scenario: Create a request-scoped provider, add instructions and a prompt, execute the request, and record token usage.
steps: Add the GenAI Client library to your Maven project dependencies.
Prepare a runtime Configurator containing provider credentials and the desired model identifier.
Resolve a provider with org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider(...).
Apply optional host-side tools with org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader if the request should expose local capabilities.
Add instructions and prompts, then call perform().
Record usage data with org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.UsageStatistics after the request completes.
examples.snippet:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.machanism.machai</groupId>
  <artifactId>genai-client</artifactId>
  <version>1.2.0-SNAPSHOT</version>
</dependency>

org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator conf = null;
org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.Genai provider = org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getProvider("OpenAI:gpt-4o-mini", conf);
org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader tools = new org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader();
tools.applyTools(provider, java.lang.Object.class);
provider.instructions("You are a helpful assistant.");
provider.prompt("Summarize this repository.");
java.lang.String answer = provider.perform();
examples.description: This is the most common how-to-use-it scenario for the library. Install the Maven dependency, configure credentials and model selection externally, optionally attach local tools, and execute a request through the common Genai contract without coupling application code to a single provider SDK.

examples.title: Generate embeddings for semantic search
examples.scenario: Create an embedding-capable provider and transform application text into vectors that can be stored in a semantic-search index.
steps: Install the library as a Maven dependency.
Configure an embedding model supported by your backend, such as an OpenAI-compatible embedding model.
Resolve an embedding provider with org.machanism.machai.ai.manager.GenaiProviderManager.getEmbeddingProvider(...).
Call embedding(text, dimensions) for every document, chunk, or query string you want to index or compare.
Store the resulting vectors in your vector store or similarity engine.
examples.description: A practical setup for retrieval and semantic search. The library hides provider-specific embedding APIs and returns a plain Java list of doubles that can be persisted or compared in downstream indexing infrastructure.

examples.title: Expose Java tools to a provider with ServiceLoader discovery
examples.scenario: Create a tool pack, let FunctionToolsLoader discover it, and make local Java capabilities available to a model during a request.
steps: Implement org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionTools in your application.
Annotate tool methods with @org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.Tool and parameters with @org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.Param.
Register the implementation through Java ServiceLoader metadata in META-INF/services.
Create a provider and then instantiate org.machanism.machai.ai.tools.FunctionToolsLoader.
Call applyTools(provider, appClass) before perform() so the request can use the registered local tools.
examples.description: This scenario shows the end-to-end path for practical host-side tool usage: implement a tool pack, let the loader discover it, register it with a provider, and then execute a request where the model may call the registered tools.

examples.title: Run a YAML-described host tool invocation with ToolsProvider
examples.scenario: Use the built-in tools-oriented provider to execute a registered host function from a structured YAML request.
steps: Instantiate org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.tools.ToolsProvider.
Register or expose the desired tool functions through the provider lifecycle.
Configure the provider for YAML-based execution mode if required by your application runtime.
Submit a prompt whose content is a YAML object containing a tool name and params block.
Call perform() and consume the returned tool result.
examples.description: A concrete how-to-use-it scenario for deterministic tool execution without a remote provider. This is useful for structured orchestration, testing, or workflows that interpret the last prompt as a YAML tool-call description.

examples.title: Configure and use a CodeMie-backed provider
examples.scenario: Install the library, configure CodeMie credentials, initialize the provider, and delegate requests to a supported downstream model family.
steps: Add the Maven dependency for org.machanism.machai:genai-client:1.2.0-SNAPSHOT.
Set runtime configuration values for GENAI_USERNAME, GENAI_PASSWORD, and optionally AUTH_URL if you need a non-default token endpoint.
Choose a supported model family such as gpt-*, gemini-*, or claude-* according to the javadoc description.
Instantiate org.machanism.machai.ai.provider.codemie.CodeMieProvider and call init(model, conf).
Send prompts or embedding requests through the delegated provider interface.
examples.description: This practical scenario demonstrates installation, configuration, and runtime use of the CodeMie integration. The provider handles OAuth 2.0 token acquisition and routes requests to the appropriate downstream implementation based on the configured model family.
